examples.explanation: Start: inMST=[True,False,False,False], minEdge=[0,10,6,5]. Step1: Find min minEdge not in MST: vertex3 with weight5. Add edge(0,3,5). Update inMST[3]=True. Update minEdge: For v=1: min(10,15)=10, v=2: min(6,4)=4. Now minEdge=[0,10,4,5]. Step2: Find min: vertex2 with weight4. Add edge(2,3,4). Update inMST[2]=True. Update minEdge: v=1: min(10,inf)=10. Now minEdge=[0,10,4,5]. Step3: Find min: vertex1 with weight10. Add edge(0,1,10). Update inMST[1]=True. MST complete with edges (0,3,5),(2,3,4),(0,1,10), total weight 19.

Start: inMST=[True,False,False,False,False], minEdge=[0,2,inf,6,inf]. Step1: Find min: vertex1 weight2. Add edge(0,1,2). Update inMST[1]=True. Update minEdge: v=2: min(inf,3)=3, v=3: min(6,8)=6, v=4: min(inf,5)=5. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step2: Find min: vertex2 weight3. Add edge(1,2,3). Update inMST[2]=True. Update minEdge: v=4: min(5,7)=5. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step3: Find min: vertex4 weight5. Add edge(1,4,5). Update inMST[4]=True. Update minEdge: v=3: min(6,9)=6. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step4: Find min: vertex3 weight6. Add edge(0,3,6). Update inMST[3]=True. MST complete with edges (0,1,2),(1,2,3),(1,4,5),(0,3,6), total weight 16.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force O(V^2)
approaches.summary: Iterate through all vertices and edges repeatedly to find the minimum weight edge connecting the growing MST to new vertices, without using any data structures for optimization. It is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum Spanning Tree Using Prim's Brute Force O(V²) Approach
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Prim's algorithm without optimization data structures, finding MST by repeatedly selecting minimum weight edges connecting to the growing tree.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Prim's algorithm implementation using the brute force O(V²) approach. The intuition is to grow a minimum spanning tree (MST) from an arbitrary starting vertex by repeatedly adding the cheapest edge that connects a vertex in the MST to a vertex outside it. Without using priority queues or adjacency lists for optimization, we maintain two arrays: 'inMST' to track which vertices are already included, and 'minEdge' to store the minimum weight edge connecting each vertex to the current MST. At each step, we scan all vertices not yet in MST to find the one with the smallest minEdge value, add it to MST, then update minEdge values for all vertices based on edges from this newly added vertex. This ensures we always add the globally cheapest edge connecting the growing MST to new vertices. The algorithm runs in O(V²) time because for each of V vertices we add, we scan all V vertices to find the minimum and update minEdge values. Space complexity is O(V) for the two arrays. Connection to L1: This full solution calls the key subroutine of finding the next vertex to add. Common mistakes include not initializing minEdge properly (use infinity for unreachable), forgetting to mark the start vertex as in MST, and incorrect edge weight comparisons. Real code pattern: Initialize inMST[0]=True, minEdge[0]=0, others=infinity. For i in range(V-1): find vertex u with min minEdge not in MST, add u to MST, for each vertex v not in MST: if graph[u][v] < minEdge[v]: update minEdge[v]=graph[u][v].

examples.trace: Start: inMST=[True,False,False,False], minEdge=[0,10,6,5]. Step1: Find min minEdge not in MST: vertex3 with weight5. Add edge(0,3,5). Update inMST[3]=True. Update minEdge: For v=1: min(10,15)=10, v=2: min(6,4)=4. Now minEdge=[0,10,4,5]. Step2: Find min: vertex2 with weight4. Add edge(2,3,4). Update inMST[2]=True. Update minEdge: v=1: min(10,inf)=10. Now minEdge=[0,10,4,5]. Step3: Find min: vertex1 with weight10. Add edge(0,1,10). Update inMST[1]=True. MST complete with edges (0,3,5),(2,3,4),(0,1,10), total weight 19.

Start: inMST=[True,False,False,False,False], minEdge=[0,2,inf,6,inf]. Step1: Find min: vertex1 weight2. Add edge(0,1,2). Update inMST[1]=True. Update minEdge: v=2: min(inf,3)=3, v=3: min(6,8)=6, v=4: min(inf,5)=5. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step2: Find min: vertex2 weight3. Add edge(1,2,3). Update inMST[2]=True. Update minEdge: v=4: min(5,7)=5. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step3: Find min: vertex4 weight5. Add edge(1,4,5). Update inMST[4]=True. Update minEdge: v=3: min(6,9)=6. minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5]. Step4: Find min: vertex3 weight6. Add edge(0,3,6). Update inMST[3]=True. MST complete with edges (0,1,2),(1,2,3),(1,4,5),(0,3,6), total weight 16.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Next Vertex to Add to MST with Minimum Edge Weight
ladders.desc: Scan all vertices to find the vertex not in MST with the smallest minEdge value, which determines the next edge to add.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core selection step in Prim's algorithm. The intuition is that among all vertices not yet in the MST, the one with the smallest minEdge value represents the cheapest connection from the current MST to a new vertex. We maintain a minEdge array where minEdge[v] stores the weight of the cheapest edge connecting vertex v to any vertex already in the MST (or infinity if no connection exists). By scanning all vertices and finding the minimum minEdge value among those not in MST, we identify the next vertex to add and the corresponding edge to include. The algorithm: Initialize minWeight = infinity, selectedVertex = -1. For each vertex v from 0 to V-1: if v is not in MST and minEdge[v] < minWeight, update minWeight = minEdge[v], selectedVertex = v. Return selectedVertex. Time complexity is O(V) per call since we examine each vertex. Space is O(1) for variables. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called V-1 times in the main algorithm to build the MST. Connection to L2: This implements the greedy selection principle. Common mistakes include not handling the case where no vertex is found (shouldn't happen in connected graphs), comparing incorrectly with infinity, and forgetting to exclude vertices already in MST. Real code pattern: min_weight = float('inf'), selected = -1; for v in range(V): if not inMST[v] and minEdge[v] < min_weight: min_weight = minEdge[v]; selected = v; return selected.

examples.trace: Scan vertices: v=0 in MST, skip. v=1: not in MST, minEdge[1]=10, current min=inf, update min=10, selected=1. v=2: not in MST, minEdge[2]=6 < 10, update min=6, selected=2. v=3: not in MST, minEdge[3]=5 < 6, update min=5, selected=3. Final selected vertex is 3 with minEdge value 5.

Scan vertices: v=0 in MST, skip. v=1 in MST, skip. v=2: not in MST, minEdge[2]=3, current min=inf, update min=3, selected=2. v=3: not in MST, minEdge[3]=6 > 3, no update. v=4: not in MST, minEdge[4]=5 > 3, no update. Final selected vertex is 2 with minEdge value 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Selection of Minimum Weight Edge Connecting MST to New Vertex
ladders.desc: The fundamental principle of always choosing the cheapest edge that expands the MST while maintaining a tree structure.
ladders.explanation: This is the core greedy principle behind Prim's algorithm. The intuition is that to build a minimum spanning tree, at each step we should add the cheapest possible edge that connects a vertex already in our growing tree to a vertex outside it. This works because of the cut property: for any cut of the graph (partition of vertices into two sets), the minimum weight edge crossing the cut is part of some MST. Our growing MST and the remaining vertices form a cut, and by always selecting the minimum edge crossing this cut, we ensure we're building an MST. The algorithm maintains a set of vertices in MST and for each vertex outside, tracks the minimum edge weight to any vertex inside. The greedy step selects the vertex outside with the smallest such weight, adding the corresponding edge. This ensures we never add an edge that would create a cycle (since we only connect to vertices outside MST) and always choose optimally locally, which leads to global optimality. Time complexity consideration: This logic requires comparing all potential edges at each step. Space: O(V) to track connections. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented by scanning all vertices to find the minimum. Connection to L3: This relies on understanding graph cuts and edge weights. Common mistakes include trying to add edges between vertices both already in MST (creates cycles), not updating connection weights properly after adding a vertex, and misunderstanding why greedy works here. Real code pattern: The essence is: while MST has < V vertices: find vertex u not in MST with smallest minEdge[u]; add u to MST; for each vertex v not in MST: if edge(u,v) exists and weight < minEdge[v]: update minEdge[v].

examples.input: Current MST vertices: {0}, vertices outside: {1,2,3}, minEdge: [0,10,6,5]
examples.trace: We have a cut between MST set {0} and outside set {1,2,3}. Edges crossing cut: (0,1,10), (0,2,6), (0,3,5). Minimum weight edge is (0,3,5). So we select vertex 3 to add to MST via edge (0,3,5). This follows the greedy principle of choosing cheapest connection.

examples.input: Current MST vertices: {0,3}, vertices outside: {1,2}, minEdge: [0,10,4,5]
examples.trace: Cut between MST {0,3} and outside {1,2}. Possible connections: from 0 to 1 weight10, from 0 to 2 weight6, from 3 to 1 weight15, from 3 to 2 weight4. Minimum is weight4 via edge (3,2). So we select vertex 2 to add via edge (3,2,4). Even though (0,2,6) exists, (3,2,4) is cheaper because minEdge was updated after adding vertex 3.

testCases.input: MST vertices: {0,1}, outside: {2,3}, connections: minEdge[2]=4 (from 1), minEdge[3]=6 (from 0)
MST vertices: {0,2}, outside: {1,3}, connections: minEdge[1]=5 (from 0), minEdge[3]=2 (from 2)
MST vertices: {0,1,3}, outside: {2,4}, connections: minEdge[2]=3 (from 1), minEdge[4]=8 (from 3)
MST vertices: {0,2,4}, outside: {1,3,5}, connections: minEdge[1]=6 (from 0), minEdge[3]=4 (from 2), minEdge[5]=9 (from 4)
MST vertices: {0,1,2,4}, outside: {3,5}, connections: minEdge[3]=5 (from 2), minEdge[5]=7 (from 4)
MST vertices: {0,3,5}, outside: {1,2,4}, connections: minEdge[1]=10 (from 0), minEdge[2]=8 (from 3), minEdge[4]=12 (from 5)
MST vertices: {0,2,3,6}, outside: {1,4,5}, connections: minEdge[1]=9 (from 0), minEdge[4]=6 (from 3), minEdge[5]=11 (from 6)
MST vertices: {0,1,4,5}, outside: {2,3,6}, connections: minEdge[2]=7 (from 1), minEdge[3]=5 (from 4), minEdge[6]=10 (from 5)

ladders.title: Building Block — Maintaining Minimum Edge Weights from MST to Each Vertex
ladders.desc: Track for each vertex the weight of the cheapest edge connecting it to the current MST, updating as new vertices are added.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves maintaining and updating the minEdge array, which is crucial for Prim's greedy selection. The intuition is that as we add new vertices to the MST, we may discover cheaper connections to remaining vertices through these newly added vertices. Initially, for the starting vertex, minEdge is 0, and for others it's infinity (or a large number). When we add a new vertex u to MST, we examine all edges from u to vertices v not yet in MST. If the weight of edge (u,v) is less than the current minEdge[v], we update minEdge[v] to this new lower weight. This ensures minEdge[v] always stores the minimum weight among all edges from any vertex in MST to v. The algorithm: After adding vertex u to MST: for each vertex v not in MST: if there's an edge between u and v with weight w: if w < minEdge[v]: minEdge[v] = w. Time complexity is O(V) per update since in worst case we check all vertices. Space is O(V) for the array. Connection to L2: This maintenance enables the greedy selection to always consider the best possible connections. Connection to L4: This involves comparing and updating numerical values. Common mistakes include not initializing minEdge properly (use infinity for unreachable), not checking if vertices are already in MST before updating, forgetting to update after each addition, and incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: After adding vertex u: for v in range(V): if not inMST[v] and graph[u][v] != 0: if graph[u][v] < minEdge[v]: minEdge[v] = graph[u][v].

examples.input: Add vertex u=3 to MST, current minEdge=[0,10,6,5], graph edges from 3: to 1 weight15, to 2 weight4
examples.trace: Vertex 3 added. Check vertices not in MST: v=1: not in MST, edge(3,1) weight15, current minEdge[1]=10, 15>10 so no update. v=2: not in MST, edge(3,2) weight4, current minEdge[2]=6, 4<6 so update minEdge[2]=4. v=3 is in MST, skip. Final minEdge becomes [0,10,4,5].

examples.input: Add vertex u=1 to MST, current minEdge=[0,2,3,6,5], graph edges from 1: to 2 weight3, to 3 weight8, to 4 weight5
examples.trace: Vertex 1 added. Check vertices: v=2: not in MST, edge(1,2) weight3, current minEdge[2]=3, 3 not < 3 so no update. v=3: not in MST, edge(1,3) weight8, current minEdge[3]=6, 8>6 so no update. v=4: not in MST, edge(1,4) weight5, current minEdge[4]=5, 5 not < 5 so no update. v=0 and v=1 in MST, skip. minEdge unchanged: [0,2,3,6,5].

testCases.input: Add u=0, minEdge=[0,inf,inf], edges from 0: to1 weight5, to2 weight3
Add u=2, minEdge=[0,5,3], edges from 2: to1 weight4
Add u=1, minEdge=[0,4,3,inf], edges from 1: to3 weight6
Add u=3, minEdge=[0,7,5,0], edges from 3: to1 weight8, to2 weight2
Add u=4, minEdge=[0,9,6,4,0], edges from 4: to1 weight7, to2 weight5, to3 weight3

Add u=3, minEdge=[0,2,7,1,9], edges from 3: to1 weight4, to2 weight6, to4 weight8
Add u=6, minEdge=[0,5,3,8,6,4,0], edges from 6: to1 weight7, to2 weight2, to3 weight9, to4 weight5, to5 weight6

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Update Minimum Values in an Array
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing a new value with an existing minimum and updating if the new value is smaller.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is at the heart of maintaining the minEdge array. The intuition is simple: we want to keep track of the smallest value seen so far for each vertex. When we encounter a potential new connection with weight w to vertex v, we compare w with the current minEdge[v]. If w is smaller, we update minEdge[v] to w; otherwise, we leave it unchanged. This ensures minEdge[v] always stores the minimum weight among all edges considered so far that connect to vertex v from the MST. The algorithm: For a given vertex v and new weight w: if w < minEdge[v]: then minEdge[v] = w. This is a constant-time operation. Space is O(1) for the comparison. Connection to L3: This operation is performed repeatedly when updating minEdge after adding a vertex. Connection to L5: This relies on understanding numerical comparison. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operator (<= instead of <, which might cause unnecessary updates), not handling infinity properly (infinity should be treated as larger than any finite number), and type mismatches. Real code pattern: if w < minEdge[v]: minEdge[v] = w. In code, ensure minEdge is initialized with float('inf') for unreachable vertices.

examples.trace: Compare w=4 with current minEdge[2]=6. Since 4 < 6, we update minEdge[2] to 4. If w were 7, we would not update because 7 is not less than 6.
infinity represents no connection yet. Since any finite number is less than infinity, we update minEdge[1] to 10. If later we find weight 5, we'll update again because 5 < 10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Weighted Graphs and Edge Representation
ladders.desc: Basic knowledge of graphs with numerical weights on edges and how to represent them for algorithm input.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept needed before tackling Prim's algorithm. A weighted graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting them, where each edge has a numerical weight representing cost, distance, or similar metric. For MST problems, we typically consider undirected graphs (edges work both ways) with positive weights. The graph can be represented in several ways; for the brute force O(V²) approach, we often use an adjacency matrix: a V×V matrix where graph[i][j] stores the weight of edge between vertex i and j, or 0 if no edge exists. The intuition is that this provides direct O(1) access to check if an edge exists and its weight, though it uses O(V²) space. Key concepts: vertices are numbered 0 to V-1, edges are pairs of vertices with weights, and the graph must be connected (all vertices reachable) for an MST to exist. Time/space for this concept alone is about understanding the representation. Connection to L4: This provides the input data (graph matrix) for comparing edge weights. Common mistakes include confusing directed vs undirected graphs (MST typically uses undirected), not handling missing edges properly (use 0 or infinity), and misunderstanding weight significance (lower is better for MST). Real code pattern: V = number of vertices; initialize graph = [[0]*V for _ in range(V)]; for each edge (u,v,w): graph[u][v]=w; graph[v][u]=w (for undirected).

examples.trace: Create 3×3 matrix initialized to 0. Edge (0,1,5): set graph[0][1]=5 and graph[1][0]=5. Edge (1,2,3): set graph[1][2]=3 and graph[2][1]=3. Edge (0,2,10): set graph[0][2]=10 and graph[2][0]=10. All other entries remain 0 indicating no edge.
Create 4×4 matrix. Edge (0,2,6): set graph[0][2]=6, graph[2][0]=6. Edge (1,3,4): set graph[1][3]=4, graph[3][1]=4. Edge (2,3,1): set graph[2][3]=1, graph[3][2]=1. Others are 0.
